text: CASTERTON, a township in the p. and 2 m. NE of Kirkby-Lonsdale, in Westmoreland. Area 4,320 acres. Pop. 623.
CASTERTON (GREAT), a parish in Rutlandshire, 2½ m. NNW of Stamford. Area 1,590 acres. Pop. 376.
CASTERTON (LITTLE), a parish adjoining the above. Area 1,450 acres. Pop. 132.

CASTETS, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, arrond. of Dax. The cant. comprises 9 com., with a pop. in 1831 of 8,395.— has been estimated at about 50,000 sq. m. It has, CAS The com. and town are 14 m. NNW of Dax. Pop. 1,446.
CASTETS-EN-DORTHE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Langon, 10 m. N of Bazas. Pop. 1,182.
CASTIFAO, a canton and commune of France, in the island of Corsica, arrond, of Corte.—The cant., comprising 4 com., had a pop. of 2,583 in 1831.—The com., 15 m. N of Corte, has a pop. of

CASTIGLIONE, a town of Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, deleg. of Lodi-et-Crema, 12 m. SE of Lodi, near the r. bank of the Adda. Pop. 2,500.— Also a small town of Lucca, 18 m. NNW of Borgoa-Mozzano, on a stream of the same name.—Also a town of Naples, in Calabria-Ultra 2da, 10 m. WNW of Nicastro, near the Adriatic. Pop. 3,300.-Also a town of Sicily, at the foot of Ætna, near the Cantara, 30 m. NNE of Catania. Pop. 2,847.
CASTIGLIONE-FIBOCCHI, a parish and village of Tuscany, in the comp. and 6 m. NW of Arezzo. Pop. 471.
CASTIGLIONE-FIORENTINO, a town of Tuscany, in the comp. and 10 m. N of Arezzo. Pop. 5,718. A theological seminary, and an episcopal college, exist here.
CASTIGLIONE-DE'-GARFAGNANA, a town of Modena, on the Serchio, 3 m. N of Castelnuovo. Pop. 334.
CASTIGLIONE-DE'-GATI, a town in the Statesof-the-Church, in the leg. and 27 m. SSW of Bologna. Pop. 2,200.
CASTIGLIONE-DEL-LAGO, a town in the States-of-the-Church, in the deleg. and 16 m. WNW of Perouse.
CASTIGLIONE-MESSER-MARINO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo-Citra, 24 m. SW of Vasto. Pop. 3,485. It contains 5 churches and a convent.
CASTIGLIONE-D'OSCIA, a parish and town of Tuscany, in the comp. of Sienna, 10 m. SE of Montalcino, on the l. bank of the Orcia. Pop. 844.

CASTIGLIONE-DELLA-PESCAJA, a town of Tuscany, in the comp. and 12 m. W of Grosseto, at the embouchure of a canal leading into the lagune of Pescaja, a salt lake about 5 m. in length. It has a small port; and exports wood, charcoal, potash, and salt.

CASTIGLIONE-DELLE-STEVIERE, a town of Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, deleg. and 20 m. NW of Mantua. Pop. 5,300. It has a citadel. The Austrians were defeated by the French under Bonaparte in the neighbourhood of this place on the 29th of June, 1796.

CASTIGLIONE-UBERTINI, a parish and village of Tuscany, in the comp. and 18 m. SW of Arezzo. Pop. 351. CASTILE [Span. Castilla; Fr. Castille], an ancient central division of Spain, in early times an independent kingdom, lying between the Gothic kingdom of Leon on the NE, and the Arabian caliphat of Cordova on the SW; but which, by gradual accessions, ultimately embraced Leon, Asturias, Galicia, Estremadura, and Andalusia; while the kingdom of Aragon comprised the E provinces of Spain. In 1471, the crowns of C. and Aragon were united by the marriage of Isabella, queen of C., with Ferdinand, king of Aragon. Bounded on the N by the bay of Biscay, if the prov. of Santander be included within its limits; on the E by Biscay, Alava, Navarre, Aragon, and Valencia; on the S by Murcia and Andalusia; and on the W by Estremadura, Leon and Asturias, the entire length of C. from N to S is about 300 m.; while its greatest breadth, under the parallel of 39°, is about 160 m.; and its total area * however, been from early times divided into two provinces, distinguished from each other as Old and New C.; the former comprising that portion which lies to the N of the Sierra de Guadarrama, the latter, that which lies to the S of this range, and which was last won from the Moors. These two provinces, with Leon, form two distinct captain-generalships; but in financial, administrative, and judicial matters, they have been subdivided into 17 provinces, of which the area and supposed pop., as given in a royal decree of 3d August, 1837, are as follows: I. NEW CASTILE. Pop. Area. 1,315 sq. m. I. NEW CASTILE, CASTILLA-LA-NUEVA, lies between the parallels of 38° 15' and 41° 20' N, and the meridians of 1° and 5° 20' W. From N to S, on the meridian of 2° 30', it measures 175 geog. m.; and from E to W, along the parallel of 40°, 200 m. It is bounded on the N by Old C.; on the E by Aragon and Valencia; on the S by Murcia and Andalusia; and on the W by Estremadura. Its area is estimated above at 30,882 sq. m. In the Encyclopædia Metropolitana, it is estimated at 31,193 sq. m.; and in the Almanach de Gotha for 1850 at 30,854 sq. m. The superficial area of the different provinces comprised in New C., and their pop. in 1833, are thus given in the Almanach de Gotha: Pop. Area. 61.88 German sq. m. 320,000 Madrid, Toledo, . . Physical features.] This prov. may be generally described as forming a great central table-land, of secondary rock formation, and of which the prevailing declination is from NE to SW. Its upland mesas or plains are literally "inland steppes, burnt up by summer-sun, and tempest and wind-rent during winter" [Ford.] On the N, the frontiers are defined oy a mountain chain, which, commencing on the NW in the Sierra-Gredos, is continued in the Sierra-Guadarrama, Sierra-Somo, Sierra-Ayllon, Sierra-Pela, to the Mata, between which and the Sierra Molina, on the E frontier, runs an elevated plateau, across which the great high roal from Saragossa to Madrid passes. On the E, the Sierra-Molina, and its S prolongation the Sierra-de-Albarracin, divide C. from Aragon; and high lands, prolonged from the latter sierra to the Sierra-Alcaraz, on the S, divide the waters flowing SE into the Mediterranean, from those which flow N to the Guadiana. On the S frontier, the Sierra-Alcaraz is continued in the Sierra-Morena. On the W, the ridges of the Sierra-de-Guadalupe cover all the country between the Guadiana and the Tagus. The basins of these two rivers, as they ex- CASTILE. tend towards the E, are divided by a nearly continuous ridge of high ground, which running NE to the Sierra-de-Albarracin, nearly divides New C. into two equal portions.—The prov. forms the upper portion of three large river-basins: viz., that of the Tagus, to which the Tajuna, Henares, Manzanares, Guadarrama, and Alberche, belong; that of the Guadiana, to which the Zancara, Geguela, Bullaque, Estena, Guadarranque, Guadlopejo, Jabalos, and Azuer, belong; and that of the Jucar, or Xucar, which comprises the Cabriel, and the Jara. The following is a list of altitudes in New C., according to a table compiled by Don F. Bauza: rice - grounds of parts of Valencia and Andalucia. Rain, indeed, is so comparatively scarce on this tableland, that the annual quantity on an average does not amount to more than 10 inches. The least quantity falls in the mountain-regions near Guadalupe, and on the high plains of Cuenca and Murcia, where sometimes eight or nine months pass without a drop falling. The occasional thunder-storms do but just lay the dust, since here moisture dries up quicker even than woman's tears. The face of the earth is tanned, tawny, and baked into a veritable terra cotta; everything seems dead and burnt on a funeral pile. It is all but a miracle how the principle of life in the green herb is preserved, since the very grass appears scorched and dead; yet when once the rain sets in, vegetation springs up, phonix-like, from the ashes, and bursts forth in an inconceivable luxuriance and dry earth drinks in these genial showers, and then rising like a giant refreshed with wine, puts forth all its strength; and what vegetation is, where moisture is combined with great heat, cannot even be guessed at in lands of stinted suns. The periods of rains are the winter and spring, and when these are plentiful, all kinds of grain, and in many places wine, are produced in abundance." [Ford.] The height of the plateau of C. reduces the mean temp. here to 59°; while on the coasts under the same parallels it ranges from 65° to 95°. The seasons are very different on the two sides of the Sierra-Morena. State of agriculture.] The rivers of New C. are well-adapted for the purposes of irrigation; and might be converted into valuable sources of fertility. The soil, though rich and fertile, is much parched, and requires nothing but moisture to adapt it to every species of agriculture; but the indolence of the pop. is a bar to all improvement. The rivers are allowed to flow undisturbed in their native channels; and though the beneficial consequences of irrigation in this prov. were long ago ascertained by Michael Alvarez Ozorio, and it was demonstrated by his son, in 1687, that even the little river of Narez was adequate to the fertilization of 80,000 fanegas of land, yet agriculture here still remains nearly in the same state in which it existed two centuries ago. Many of the pueblos are under tolerable cultivation; but others are completely neglected, though capable of great fecundity. Among the former are those in the country lying between Guadalaxara and Alcala-de-Henarez, the district of Torrelaguno, the rich plain of Requena, and the vale of Aranjuez; and among the latter are the lands commencing on the frontiers of Aragon, and extending for 20 leagues to Torrija; also those between the Bravo and the Alberche, and the country near Alcorcon, on the road leading from Madrid to Talavera-de-la-Reyna.-Wheat is the staple produce of the province; with the exception of a little barley, it is the only grain cultivated. It yields abundant crops, wherever its culture is made an object of attention. In the valleys lying beneath the Sierra-de-Cuenca, and in the environs of Madrid and some other towns, as well as in the plain of Talavera-de-la-Reyna, &c., the harvests are most luxuriant; yet the quantity produced by the whole province is scarcely sufficient for the consumption of the inhabitants. Very little flax is raised, though the soil is well-adapted to its cultivation hemp, however, is more plentiful, particularly in Alcaria; and the district of Huete alone produces yearly the average quantity of 5,000 or 6,000 arobas. Saffron is planted in various parts of New C., particularly between Madrid and Cuenca. It is in great demand over all Spain, especially in the Castilian provinces; and consequently is considered a profitable object of cultivation. Its consumption, however, in some places, has greatly diminished; and the 40 quintals which were formerly produced in Huete are now reduced to 5.—The greater part of this prov. is equally destitute of foliage as that of Old C. Woods are rare; even for several leagues round the capital a tree is scarcely to be seen. An extensive tract of 40 leagues, from Aranjuez to the confines of Valencia, is almost without a shrub or leaf to break the naked uniformity. The fertile plain between Guadalaxara and Alcala de Henarez is devoid of trees; and the country through which the great road leading from Portugal lies is equally naked. "There is not a tree to be seen," says Mr. Swinburne, "from the Sierra-Morena to Toledo, nor from the banks of the Tagus to Madrid: a few dwarfish evergreen oaks, huddled together in nooks of hills, and some stumpy olive plants, scarcely deserve the name of trees." A few particular spots, however, are more favoured. In the plain of Requena, trees are cultivated with considerable care and success; and some of the mountains, particularly those of Cuenca, are clothed with indigenous pines, and various kinds of oak. Poplars are abundant at Talavera-de-la-Reyna; and there are woods of green oak in the environs of Torrija. The banks of the rivers are in general embellished with elms, poplars, and willows; and between the village of Flores and the river Henarez, is a forest of the Quercus coccifera, producing the kermes worm which supplies the carnation tint. Not only the fallowland, but even fields under the plough, are often seen full of broom plants, which here attain great luxuriance. Fruit-trees are scarce; and though the soil, with the means of irrigation possessed, is favourable for their culture, yet this prov. draws most of its fruits from Valencia and Aragon. Vines are cultivated in several places; but the wine of New C., though good, is often thick, and sometimes hard, and is considered as inferior to that of Aragon and the southern provinces. Madrid is supplied with wine grown at Tarançon, Arganda, and other places in its immediate vicinity. In some cantons, plantations of olives are both numerous and flourishing, especially between CASTILE. Cebolla and the Guadarrama, and the southern parts of the province. The fruit is excellent, but the oil both of New C. and Aragon is execrable, owing to the method of preparing it. Considerable quantities of honey are gathered in this prov.; that of Alcaria and the Sierra-de-Cuenca is reckoned the best in Spain. On the mountains of Cuenca alone, the produce of 1773 was 3,334 arrobas of honey, and 156 of wax. Considerable numbers of black cattle, sheep, and mules are reared. Game is abundant; and bears and lynxes are occasionally seen in the mountainrecesses. Manufactures.] The manufactures of New C. consist chiefly in woollen stuffs. The cloths of Brihuega are excellent in their quality; but they are surpassed by those of Guadalaxara and Vigonia. Very little linen is made here; its manufacture is confined almost entirely to Toledo and St. Ildefonso. Extensive manufactories of silks, however, are established at Requena, Toledo, and Talavera-de-la-Reyna; where are also fabricated plain and figured velvets, of mixed colours and embroidered with gold; taffetas, satins, silk serges, silk stuffs, gold and silver stuffs, and silk ribbons. At Requena these manufactures formerly gave employment to nearly 800 looms; and the annual consumption of the three places was estimated at 100,000 lbs. of silk, 4,000 merks of silver, and 70 merks of gold. Besides these, the other principal branches of manufacture are calicoes, laces, and tapestries; silk, cotton, and worsted stockings; hats and caps; porcelain and delft-ware; pikes, swords, and cutlery ware. Most of its manufactures, however, are scarcely adequate to supply the demands of the prov. itself; and it is indebted to other districts for many articles of comfort or luxury. A few of its stuffs are carried to Seville and Cadiz, and thence exported to America; but its cloths and woollens are inferior to those of England and France. Its silks, which are exposed to sale at Madrid, are also undersold by the French silks, as well as by those of Catalonia and Valencia. There are iron-mines and iron-works in the prov. of Cuenca.-Considerable improvements have been made on the roads of this province, which have facilitated the transportation of goods between the different districts. Some of these roads, especially that from Madrid to France, by Burgos and Irun, are broad and handsome; and they are occasionally planted with trees on each side. Many of the cross roads, however, are still scarcely passable, being rough, narrow, and often dangerous; and even some of the main lines are much neglected. Population.] The pop. of New C. bears no proportion to its extent; and according to Laborde, has been sensibly diminishing during the last century. Of many flourishing villages, scarcely a vestige remains; and, when he wrote, 195 deserted chapels perpetuated the names of as many once populous hamlets, which had long been left without an inhabitant. The Castilian is the standard-dialect of the Spanish language. Though New C. is the residence of the court, and enjoys a fertile soil and a salubrious climate, yet its general characteristics are those of poverty and ignorance. Industry, science, and the liberal arts, are confined entirely to the capital; but the luxury, wealth, and activity of Madrid emit scarcely one ray beyond its walls. The means of instruction are denied to the majority of the inhabitants, and but niggardly supplied to others.-"The New Castilian," says Laborde, "possesses qualities of genuine excellence; he is honourable and humane, sober and temperate, and revolts from every species of falsehood and duplicity. In his temper he is more docile than the native of Old C., who pertinaciously retains the inflexibility of his ancestors, whilst the other readily assimilates with the character of the ! neighbouring provinces. In general, the observer may trace in him a complexional resemblance to the country he approximates; he is most civilized in the environs of Madrid; most useful in the borders of Andalucia; most active and industrious on the confines of Valencia; most arrogant and rude on the frontiers of Aragon and the Sierra-de-Cuenca; most indolent in the neighbourhood of Estremadura; whilst generosity, nobleness, and benevolence, are the bonds which unite him to Old C." Guadalaxara, Jaen, Madrid, Toledo, and Valencia. chief places are Madrid, Toledo, Cuenca, Talaveracala-de-Henares. II. OLD CASTILE, or CASTILLA-LA-VIEJA, is bounded on the N by the bay of Biscay, if the prov. of Santander be reckoned within its limits; on the E by the Basque provinces, Navarre, and Aragon; on the S by New Castile; and on the W by Leon. Its general figure is triangular. In the latitude of Burgos, it has a breadth of about 110 m.; but towards its apex or northern extremity it narrows to 20 m. From N to S, its length is about 200 m. Its area and pop. in 1833 are thus given in the Almanach de Gotha for 1850: Pop. Area. 224,407 Burgos, . 161·13 German sq. m. 147,718 Logrona, Physical features.] The surface of Old C. is still more diversified than that of New C. It presents "a succession of plains, or rather one expanse of down, surrounded by lofty mountains, and occasionally intersected by other mountains of equal elevation, and diversified by hills, eminences, and gentle acclivities." Its northern mountains are ramifications from the great Pyrenean ridge, which runs along the N provinces of Spain as far as the Atlantic, and consist chiefly of the mountains of Burgos and the llamadas of Santander. The Sierrad'Oca with its continuation the Sierra-Neyla, the Sierra-Cebollera, and the Sierra-Oncala, intersect the country from NW to SE, forming on their NE flank the water-shed of the basin of the Ebro. The Sierra-Oncala unites with the Sierra-Moncayo on the SE frontier of the prov. The southern frontier mountains, dividing Old and New Castile, have been noticed in the preceding article. From the Sierra- Guadarrama, the Sierra-d'Avila runs W across the S prolongation of the prov.; and the Sierra-de- Gredos [alt. 10,552 ft.] divides its SW extremity from Estremadura. - The principal rivers are the Arlançon, flowing SW to the Pisuerga; the Duero, intersecting the central part of the prov. from E to W; and the Ebro, skirting its NE frontier, and dividing it from Navarre. The Tiron, the Oja, the Nagerillo, the Yrequa, and the Cidacos belong to the basin of the Ebro, and water the NE districts. The Ucero, Rituerto, Pedro, Grado, Duraton, Eresma, and Adaja, belong to the basin of the Duero, and water the central districts. The basin of the Arlança and the Arlançon, rivers which unite their waters on the NW frontiers of Old C., is separated by the Sierra-Umbria on the S from the basin of the Duero. In the extreme SW, the water-shed of the Alberche, on the N, is formed by the Sierra-d'Avila. -The northern portion of Old Castile, in its extreme limits, or the prov. of Santander, is watered by the Azon, the Paz, the Besaya, the Nansa, and the Deba.—The following is a list of altitudes of various points in Old C., beginning on the frontiers of New C., and proceeding N to the frontiers of Santander, according to Don F. Bauza's table: Avila, Honrubia, dil massociationa di Inn of Juanilla, Sierra-Cebollera, off with the Herreria-de-los-Chorros, Climate, Soil, &c.] The climate of Old C. is cold and moist as compared with that of New C. In the elevated districts, frosts often last for three months at a time. [Ford.] The soil in some places is a fine black loam; in others it is so rocky as to be insusceptible of cultivation; but wherever there is a sufficient supply of water, it is highly productive, particularly in the district lying between Rodrigo and Burgos. There the harvests are most luxuriant, and every species of grain is in excellent quality. The ground is merely scratched with a light plough; but notwithstanding this slight preparation, such is the inherent humidity and richness of the soil, that the crops are seldom known to fail; and so plentiful is overplus of their crops, owing to the difficulties of conveyance, can seldom be converted into wealth: their only method of exportation is on the backs of mules, and the roads are in general so wretched that some of them are even scarcely practicable to these sure-footed animals. Part of the noble road which leads from Bayonne to Madrid, intersects this province, and passes through the cities of Burgos and Valladolid; but the cross roads are no better than they were four centuries ago; -narrow, steep, rugged, sometimes miry, and often impassable to horses. The pass of Guadarrama, the nearest point of communication between Old and New C., [alt. 4,657 ft.] was, till about the middle of the last cent., so steep and difficult that it could not be attempted without danger. But by the care and munificence of Ferdinand VI. a road was formed, beginning at the village of Espinar, by which the ascent was rendered safe and easy. The difficulties of exportation, however, would be almost completely obviated in this province, by the completion of the canal of Campos. This work was begun in 1753 by the same prince, and was intended to reach from Fontibre, 2 leagues from Reynosa, to the river Pisuerga, 5 m. below Valladolid, and then, passing by Segovia, to join the Duero: thus opening a communication between the Ebro and the Duero. After it had been cut for several miles, it was suddenly stopped, and it has never since been resumed, though its accomplishment would require neither extraordinary labour nor expense. Productions.] Old C. produces a considerable quantity of wine, but it is inferior both in strength and flavour to that of the southern provinces. Fruits are in general scarce, except in the district of Burela, which is covered with luxuriant orchards, and wellwooded with elms, poplars, and chestnuts. But in ] other districts, the appearance of trees is chiefly confined to the banks of the rivers. Many of the extensive plains are almost entirely bare of foliage; in some places, particularly between Cabezan and Rodrigo, they are not embellished with a single shrub. Madder is produced in great quantities in the vicinity of Burgos, Segovia, and Valladolid. Wool, however, is the staple commodity of Old C., and constitutes an important article of foreign exportation. Innumerable flocks of sheep, which winter in the plains, find rich and luxuriant pasture during summer on the mountains. Their wool is equal in quality to any in Spain; and the cloth of Segovia was at one time famous throughout Europe. Immense numbers of cows are also reared upon the mountains; but such is the ignorance or indolence of the inhabitants, that their produce is turned to little account.-While Old C. continued to be the residence of its sovereigns, it held the first rank among the provinces of Spain for opulence, as well as for the excellence and variety of its manufactures; but since the removal of the court to Madrid, its prosperity has rapidly declined. The manufactures of woollen and linen cloths for which it was formerly so distinguished are now almost annihilated. The greater part of its wool is carried to France, where it is fabricated into cloths and caps, many of which return to Spain for sale; and the half of what is manufactured at Segovia is sent to Madrid and some other places in New C. Paper, hides, and leather, are also exported to the same prov.; white glass to Aragon; and a little wine to Biscay. Towns.] The principal places in this prov. are Burgos, the capital and an archbishop's see; the episcopal cities of Osma, Siguenza, Avila, Valladolid, Segovia, Calahorra, and Soria; and the towns of Logrono, Santo - Domingo - de - la - Calzada, Granon, Almarza, Almarzan, Miranda, and Alfaro. - In Old C., as already observed, was laid the foundation of the Spanish monarchy. Its capital was the seat of the government; and notwithstanding the extension of the empire, it continued to be the royal residence till the 13th cent., after which it shared that honour with Toledo, till the beginning of the 16th cent., when Charles V. transferred his court to Madrid.
CASTILE, a township in Wyoming co., in the state of New York, U. S., on the Genesee river. 249 m. W of Albany. Pop. 2,833.
CASTILLEJA-DEL-CAMPO, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 15 m. W of Seville.
CASTILLEJA - DE - LA - CUESTA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 5 m. NW of Seville.
CASTILLEJA-DE-GUZMAN, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 2 m. N of Seville, near the r. bank of the Guadalquiver.
CASTILLEJO-DE-INJESTA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 12 m. SSE of Cuenca.
CASTILLIO. See CASTIGLIONE.
CASTILLO, an islet in the Grenadine group, in the Caribbean sea, in N lat. 12° 47', W long. 61° 16'.
CASTILLO-DE-BAYUELA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. of Toledo, 9 m. NNE of Talavera, near the r. bank of the Alberche.
CASTILLO-DE-QUIROGA, a town of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. and 48 m. SE of Lugo, on the Quiroga river.

CASTILLON, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ariege, arrond. of Saint-Gerons.—The cant., comprising 26 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 16,589. The com., 8 m. SW of Saint-Gerons, on the Lizard, has a pop. of 1,000.—Also a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. II. CAS of Gironde.—The cant. comprises 14 com., with a pop. in 1831 of 10,827.—The com. and town, on the r. bank of the Dordogne, 12 m. ESE of Libourne, had a pop. of 3,009 in 1841. It is a place of active industry, possessing nail, rope, and brick works, and linen and cotton spinning-mills. In the environs are the ruins of the chateau, in which Montaigne died. The French defeated the English under the walls of C. in 1451.
CASTILLON - DE - COURRY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Saint-Ambroix. Pop. 1,261.
CASTILLON-DEBATS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gers, cant. of Vic-Fezensac. Pop. 1,125.

CASTILLONNE'S, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, arrond. and 20 m. NNW of Villeneuve-sur-Lot .- The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,368.—The com. had a pop. of 2,028 in 1841.

CASTINE, a town and seaport in Hancock co., in the state of Maine, U.S., 34 m. S of Bangor, on the E side of Penobscot bay. It has an excellent harbour; and employs considerable shipping in the lumber trade, coasting-trade, and fisheries. Pop. 1,188.
CASTLE, a hamlet in the p. of Pentyrch, Glamorganshire. Pop. 195.
CASTLE-ACRE, a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. N of Swaffham. Area 3,210 acres. Pop. 1,495.
CASTLE ASHBY. See ASHBY CASTLE.

CASTLEBAR, or Aglish, a parish and markettown in co. Mayo. Area of p. 14,794 acres. Pop. 10,464. The surface rises in Mount Burren to an alt. of 1,299 ft. above sea-level.—The town of C. is 8½ m. ENE of Westport. It possesses a large share of the linen trade of the co. Pop. in 1831, 6,373; in 1841, 5,137. The courts of assize for the co. are held in this town.
CASTLE-BARNARD. See BARNARD-CASTLE.
CASTLE-BAY, a village in the p. of Carnew, co. Wicklow. Pop. 105.
CASTLE-BELLINGHAM, a village in the p. of Gernonstown, co. Louth. Pop. 665.
CASTLE-BLAKENEY, or KILLASOLAN, a parish and village in co. Galway. Area of p. 11,482 acres. Pop. 4,496. The v. of C. is 5\frac{3}{4} m. WNW of Ahascrach. The p. also contains the v. of Caltra.
CASTLE-BLANEY, a post and market-town in the co. of Monaghan, 91 m. SW of Newton Hamilton. Pop. 2,134. A considerable quantity of linen is made in this town and in the neighbourhood.
CASTLE-BOUY, or St. Johnstown, a parish in co. Down, 3 m. N of Portaferry. Area 1,358 acres. Pop. 775.
CASTLE-BRACK, a parish in Queen's co., 6 m. NW of Portarlington. Area 9,275 acres. Pop. 1,924. The head-stream of the Barrow skirts this p. at an alt. of 235 ft. above sea-level.
CASTLE-BRIDGE, a village in the co. of Wexford, 3\frac{1}{2} m. N of Wexford. Pop. 439.
CASTLE-BROMWICH, a hamlet in the p. of Aston, Warwickshire. Pop. 779.
CASTLE-BYTHE, or BIGH, a parish in Pembrokeshire, 10 m. NE of Haverfordwest. Pop. 284.
CASTLE-CAER-EINEON, a parish in Montgomeryshire, 41 m. WSW of Welshpool. Pop. 805.
CASTLE-CAMPS, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 5 m. ESE of Linton. Area 2,613 acres. Pop. 854.
CASTLE CARBERY, a parish and v. of co. Kildare. Area of p. 4,796 acres. Pop. 754. The v. is 3\frac{1}{4} m. E by N of Edenderry. Pop. in 1831, 159.
CASTLE-CAREY, a market-town and parish in Somerset, 113 m. SW of London. Area of p. 3,640 acres. Pop. 1,942.
CASTLE-CARLTON, a parish in Lincolnshire. Area 500 acres. Pop. 52.
CASTLE-CARROCK, a parish in Cumberland, 10½ m. E of Carlisle. Area 3,640 acres. Pop. 351.
CASTLECARY, a hamlet in the shire of Stirling, and a station of the Edinburgh and Glasgow railway; 8 m. WSW of Falkirk; 32 m. by railway from Edinburgh, and 151 m. from Glasgow.
CASTLE-CAULFIELD, a village in the p. of Donaghmore, co. Tyrone, 2 m. W of Dungannon. Pop. 167.
CASTLE-CHURCH, a parish in Staffordshire, 1 m. SW of Stafford. Area 3,460 acres. Pop. 1,484.
CASTLE-COMBE, a parish in Wiltshire, 52 m. NW of Chippenham. Area 1,770 acres. Pop. 600.

CASTLE-COMER, a parish in co. Kilkenny. Area 21,592 acres. Pop. 13,535. It contains a town of the same name, and the v. of Clough.-The town of C. is 10 m. N by E of Kilkenny, in a kind of sloping hollow on the Dinane rivulet. Pop. 1,765. There are infantry barracks here.

CASTLE-CONNELL, a parish in co. Limerick. Area 6,678 acres. Pop. 5,433. It contains a town of the same name, and the v. of Montpelier.-The town stands on the Shannon, 6 m. NW by N of Limerick. Pop. 1,106.
CASTLE-CONNOR, a parish in co. Sligo. Area 10,677 acres. Pop. 5,136. It comprises the v. of Corbally.
CASTLE-DAWSON, a village in the p. of Magherafelt, co. Londonderry, 2 m. NW of Lough Neagh. Pop. 575.
CASTLE-DERG, a village in the p. of Skirts, co. Tyrone, 7 m. W of Newton-Stewart. Pop. 476.

CASTLE-DERMOT, or Thistle-Dermot, a parish and town in co. Kildare, about 5 m. SW of Ballytore. Area of the p. 7,497 acres. Pop. 1,674.— The town of C. stands on the Lear rivulet, 5 m. S by W of Ballytore. It is a very ancient place, and contains many interesting remains of early ages.
CASTLE-DILLON, a parish in co. Kildare, 2½ m. SSW of Celbridge. Area 1,133 acres. Pop. 147.

CASTLE-DOUGLAS, a town in the p. of Kelton, in Kirkcudbrightshire, 18 m. W by S of Dumfries. Pop. in 1841, 1,847. Its name is derived from Threave castle, a ruined stronghold to the SW of the town. A grain-market is held here every Monday.
CASTLE-EATON, a parish in Wiltshire, 4½ m. NW of Highworth. Area 2,020 acres. Pop. 312.
CASTLE-EDEN, a parish in Durham, 10 m. E by S of Durham. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. 558.
CASTLE-ELLIS, a parish in co. Wexford, 2<sup>1</sup>/<sub>4</sub> m. S of Oulart. Area 5,603 acres. Pop. 1,871.
CASTLE-FINN, a market and post-town in the p. of Donaghmore, co. Donegal, 5 m. WSW of Strabane. Pop. 567.
CASTLEFORD, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. NE of Wakefield. Area 2,040 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,850, of whom 1,414 were in the township of C.
CASTLE-FROME, a parish in Herefordshire, 6½ m. NNW of Ledbury. Area 1,320 acres. Pop. 183.
CASTLE-GREGORY, a small fishing-town in the p. of Killeiny, co. Kerry, 9 m. ENE of Dingle. Pop. 504.
CASTLE-GRESLEY, a hamlet in the p. of Church-Gresley, Derbyshire, 41 m. ESE of Burtonupon-Trent. Pop. 164.

CASTLE-HAVEN, a parish in co. Cork, 41 m. ESE of Skibbereen. Area 10,542 acres. Pop. 6,056. C. bay opens from the sea with a breadth of about half-a-mile; and penetrates the land, with gradually decreasing breadth, about 3 m. northward. It diminishes in depth of water from 30 to 14 ft. at ebb; and offers good anchorage opposite C. village. The CAS bay is known to the Spaniards by the name of Porto- Castello, and is famous for a notable sea-fight, in 1602, between their admiral Don Pedro de Quibar, and Sir Richard Levison. The headland which flanks the E side of its entrance bears the name of Galleon-point, from the circumstance of the British admiral having sunk some galleons near it; and it still exhibits vestiges of intrenchments which were thrown up by the Spaniards.—A little S of Galleonpoint is an islet called Skiddy's island, or the Quince.

CASTLE-ISLAND, a parish and town in co. Kerry. Area of p. 29,585 acres. Pop. 6,260. The town, which has a pop. of 1,687, stands on the Mauy rivulet, 83 m. E by S of Tralee. It is a decayed place.—The p. also contains the v. of Scartaglin.
CASTLE-JORDAN, a parish, partly in King's co., and co. Meath, 31 m. SSW of Kinnegad. Area of the whole p. 17,371 acres. Pop. 4,079.
CASTLE KIEVAN, a village in the p. of Loughan, co. Meath. Pop. in 1831, 162.
CASTLE-KNOCK, a parish and village in co. Dublin, 4 m. WNW of Dublin. Area of p. 7,124 acres. Pop. 4,063.
CASTLE-LEAVINGTON, a township in the p. of Kirk-Leavington, N. R. of Yorkshire, 31 m. SE of Yarm. Area 730 acres. Pop. 46.
CASTLE-LOST, a parish in co. Westmeath, 6 m. WSW of Kinnegad. Area 9,450 acres. Pop. 2,058.

CASTLE-LYONS, a parish, partly in the barony of Condons and Clangibbons, partly in that of Barrymore, co. Cork. Area of the former section, 2,988 acres; of the latter, 9,720 acres. Pop. of the whole 5,536.
CASTLE-MACADAM, a parish in co. Wicklow, 43 m. S of Rathdrum. Area 10,843 acres. Pop. 5,633. The surface is a portion of the superb vale of the Ovoca.
CASTLE-MAGNER, a parish in co. Cork, 63 m. WNW of Mallow. Area 7,881 acres. Pop. 3,007.

CASTLEMAIN, a village in the p. of Kiltallagh, co. Kerry, 14 m. NNE of Milltown. Area 27 acres. Pop. 150. -There is an estuary of the same name at the head of Dingle bay, which penetrates the land about 61 m., and receives at its head the rivers Maine and Laune.
CASTLE-MARTIN, a parish in Pembrokeshire, 5 m. SW by W of Pembroke. Pop. 408.
CASTLE MARTYR, a small town in the barony of Imokilly, co. Cork, 42 m. E of Middleton. Pop. 1,397.
CASTLE MORE, a parish, partly in co. Roscommon, partly in co. Mayo, 13 m. SE of Ballaghadireen. Area 8,914 acres. Pop. 3,532.
CASTLE-MORTON, a parish in Worcestershire, 4½ m. SW by W of Upton-upon-Severn. Area 3,780 acres. Pop. 855.
CASTLE-NORTHWICH, a township in the p. of Great Budworth, Cheshire, 6 m. NNW of Middlewich. Area 100 acres. Pop. 746.
CASTLE-PLUNKET, a village in the p. of Baslick, co. Roscommon, 41 m. E by S of Castlerea. Area 12 acres. Pop. 202.
CASTLE-POLLARD, a town in the p. of Rathgraff, co. Westmeath, 11 m. WNW of Lough Lane. Area 48 acres. Pop. 1,310.
CASTLE-PRUDHOE, a township in the p. of Ovingham, in Northumberland. Pop. 369.
CASTLE-PULVERBATCH, a township in Pulverbatch p., in Shropshire. Pop. 154.
CASTLE-RAHAN, a parish at the west end of the barony of Castle Rahan, co. Cavan, 45 m. NNW of Oldcastle. Area 10,315 acres. Pop. 7,589.
CASTLEREA, a market and post-town in the p. of Kilkeevan, co. Roscommon, 131 m. NW by N of Dublin, on the river Suck. Pop. 1,233.
CASTLE-RICHARDS, a parish, partly in Herefordshire, and partly in Shropshire. Area 4,320 acres. Pop. 656.
CASTLE-RICKARD, a parish in co. Meath, 42 m. NE of Clonard. Area 3,433 acres. Pop. 551.
CASTLE-RISING, a parish and borough in Norfolk, 5 m. SE of Kingslynn. Area 2,330 acres. Pop. 358.—The town was in former ages a considerable port, but its harbour has long been choked-up.
CASTLE-SOWERBY, a parish in Cumberland, 10 m. WNW of Penrith. Area 7,940 acres. Pop. 1,007.
CASTLE-STREET, a township in the p. of St. Mary, Cumberland. Pop. 971.
CASTLE-TERRA, a parish in co. Cavan, 4 m. NNE of Cavan. Area 9,980 acres. Pop. 6,813.
CASTLE-THORPE, a parish in Buckinghamshire, 4 m. NNE of Stony-Stratford. Area 1,380 acres. Pop. 365.

CASTLETON, a parish and village in Derbyshire, 5 m. N of Tideswell. Area 10,100 acres. Jop. 1,500.—C. is a considerable village, situated at the bottom of a rugged eminence, on which is seated the ancient castle from which it derives its name. The inhabitants draw their means of subsistence principally from the mining-district by which it is surrounded. Directly below the keep of the castle, rises a precipitous edge of limestone rock; at the height of 50 ft. in the face of this rock, is the entrance to the cavern of the Peak, or, as it is vulgarly called, the Devil's cave. This astonishing excavation, wholly formed of limestone strata, abounding in marine exuviæ, with an occasional intermixture of schist, extends from the entrance to its farthest extremity, 2,300 ft., and is supposed to be 645 ft. in depth from the summit of the mountain.—About 1 m. distant from this is the Speedwell level, or Navigation mine.—A little to the W of the Speedwell level is the Odin lead-mine, which although it has been carried on for many centuries, is still one of the most productive in the county.—At a short distance from this, rising to the height of 1,300 ft. above the vale of C., is Mam-Tor, or the Mother-hill, called also, from the fragments of shale and gritstone continually falling from its southern side, the Shivering mountain.

CASTLETON, a parish in Dorsetshire, 1 m. NE of Sherborne. Area 30 acres. Pop. 113.—Also a township in Rochdale p., Lancashire, 13 m. SE of Rochdale. Pop. 14,279.—Also a v. in the p. of Olrick, in Caithness, 5 m. E of Thurso. Pop. in 1836, 311.—Also a v. in the district of Braemar, in Aberdeenshire, on the Clunie, 58 m. W of Aberdeen.

CASTLETON, a township of Rutland co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., 74 m. SSW of Montpelier. It presents a hilly surface, and is watered by a river of the same name; and on the W is lake Bombazine, the outlet of which forms good water-power. The soil is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 1,769. The village is situated on the Castleton river.—Also a village of Schodack, in Rensselaer co., state of New York, 8 m. SE of Albany, on the E bank of Hudson river. Pop. 350. - Also a township of Richmond co., in the same state, on the N end of Staten island, 155 m. S of Albany. It is hilly, but generally fertile and well-cultivated. It contains the villages of has a pop. of 1,063, is situated on the Awbeg, 7½ m. Tomkinsville and New Brighton. Pop. 2,263.

CASTLETOWN, a port and town in the island of Man, on a bay of the same name, near the S extremity of the island. Pop. in 1841, 2,283. As the seat of the governor, and of the courts of law, it is considered the metropolis of the island; but it is inferior in size and commercial importance to Douglas.

CASTLETOWN, a parish in co. Limerick, 4 m. NE of Pallas Green. Area 1,777 acres. Pop. 919. ~ CAS -Also a parish in co. Louth, 11 m. WNW of Dundalk. Area 2,611 acres. Pop. 1,043.—Also a village in the p. of Offerlane, Queen's co., 13 m. SSW of Mountrath. Area 27 acres. Pop. 403.-Also an ancient town in Killaconenagh p., co. Cork, on the W side of Bantry bay. Area 41 acres. Pop. 881. -Also a parish in co. Limerick, 41 m. SE by S of Newcastle. Area 23,2763 acres. Pop. in 1831, 10,742.

CASTLETOWN, a parish forming the S extremity of Roxburghshire, having the form of an irregular triangle, and including a more extensive area than any other parish in the south of Scotland. It is bounded on the N by the parishes of Cavers, Hobkirk, and Southdean; on the E and S by Northumberland and Cumberland; and on the W by Dumfries-shire. Its greatest length, from Fanna hill on the NE, to its S extremity at the confluence of Mare-burn with Kershope-water is 17, m.; its greatest breadth, from Peel-fell on the E to Tudhope-hill on the W, is 14 m. Pop. in 1801, 1,109; in 1841, 2,135.—In history and poetry, and frequently in conversation, it is called Liddesdale, from the river Liddel, which runs through it from E to S. The upper or northern part of the district is mountainous and bleak; but is generally dry, and affords good sheep-pasturage. Some of the mountains both here and along the W and E boundaries, are very high and precipitous. Millenwood-fell, and Windhead, are each nearly 2,000 ft. in height; and Tudhopehill is 1,830 ft., and, being seen from a great distance at sea, serves as a landmark for ships. The lower extremity of the parish, and all parts of it distant from the streams, are wild and bleak. Along the banks of the Hermitage and the Liddel, however, it is luxuriant, full of rural beauty, and occasionally picturesque. Near the head of the parish on the E, the rivers Tyne and Liddel take their rise in the midst of a vast bog, which, on account of its stagnant appearance, is called Dead-water. This secluded district was, at a former period, inhabited by tribes of freebooters, the chief of whom were the Elliotts and the Armstrongs. Their peel-houses, where they stored their booty and rallied at a moment of danger, still, in some quarters, lift their ruined heights before the eyes of a traveller as memorials of a lawless age.—The modern v. of C. is situated on the r. bank of the Liddel, 5 m. E of Cannobie. Pop. 1,030.
CASTLETOWN-ARRA, a parish in co. Tipperary, 7 m. W by N of Nenagh. Area 9,283 acres. Pop. 4,292.
CASTLETOWN-DELVIN, a parish and village in co. Meath. Area of p. 18,232 acres. Pop. 4,588. The v. is 63 m. W of Athboy.
CASTLETOWN-ELY, a parish in King's co., 3 m. SE of Moneygall. Area 1,803 acres. Pop. 371.
CASTLETOWN-KILPATRICK, a parish in co. Meath, 2 m. SSE of Nobber. Area 4,067 acres. Pop. 1,075.
CASTLETOWN-KINDELAN, a parish in co. Westmeath, 41 m. N of Kilbeggan. Area 11,398 acres. Pop. 4,290.
CASTLETOWN-ROCHE, a parish and town in co. Cork, situated to the SE from Fermoy. Area of the p. 6,485 acres. Pop. 3,476.—The town, which WNW of Fermoy.
CASTLE-TOWNSEND, a village and small seaport in the p. of Castlehaven, co. Cork, 4½ m. ESE of Skibbereen. Area 21 acres. Pop. 770.
CASTLE-VENTRY, a parish, partly in East Carberry barony, and partly in that of Ibane and Barryroe, co. Cork, 5 m. W of Clonakilty. Area 4,754 acres. Pop. 2,196.
CASTLE-WELLAN, a market and post town in the p. of Kilmegan, co. Down, 4 m. WSW of Clough. Area 25 acres. Pop. 806.
CASTLEWRIGHT, a township in the p. of Mainstone, Montgomeryshire. Pop. 173.
CASTLEY, a township in the p. of Leathley, W. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. ENE of Otley, on the N bank of the Wharfe. Area 480 acres. Pop. in 1841, 110.
CASTON, a parish of Norfolk, 4 m. SE of Watton. Area 1,680 acres. Pop. in 1841, 513.
CASTOR, a parish of Northamptonshire, 5 m. W of Peterborough, bounded on the SW by the Nen. Area 7,020 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,313.
CASTOR, a township of Madison co., in the state of Missouri, U. S. Pop. in 1840, 624.—Also a river of Newfoundland, which runs SSW, and falls into St. John's bay.
CASTOR AND POLLUX, two small islets in the gulf of Mexico, at the entrance of the bay Espiritu Santo or Tampa, in N lat. 27° 55'.
CASTRACONTRIGO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. SE of Leon, partido of Baneza. Pop. 720.
CASTRANITZA, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Macedonia, sanj. and 40 m. ESE of Monastir, on an affluent of the Potova, 7 m. SE of Ostrovo.
CASTREJON, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 55 m. N of Palencia, partido and 5 m. SW of Cervera-de-Riopisuerga, at the source of the Buedo.
CASTRELO (SANTA MARIA DE), a town of Galicia, in the prov. of Orense, partido of Celanova. Pop. 800.

CASTRES, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Tarn.—The arrond. comprises a superficies of 222,287 hectares, and 14 cant., viz.: Saint Amand-la-Bastide, Anglès, Castres, Dourgne, Abruguière, Lacaune, Lautrec, Mazamet, Montredon, Murat, Roquecourbe, Vabre, and Vielmur. Pop. in 1831, 131,154; in 1846, 143,743.—The cant. comprises 5 com. Pop. in 1831, 18,383; in 1846, 18,990.—The town is situated in a fertile valley, at an alt. of 508 ft. above sea-level, and 26 m. SSE of Albi. Pop. in 1789, 12,948; in 1821, 14,610; in 1831, 16,418; in 1836, 17,602; and in 1841, 19,225. It is intersected by the Agout, which is here crossed by two stone-bridges. The streets are narrow and tortuous, and the houses generally ill-built. The principal edifice is the episcopal palace, which now contains the prefect's house, the mayoralty, and the public library, and is surrounded by handsome gardens. There are also 2 hospitals, barracks, a bank, a theatre, a diocesan seminary, a consistorial church, and a fine promenade. This town is celebrated for the variety of its manufactures, consisting in cloth, duffel, ratteen, and other woollen fabrics, linen, calico, silk, &c. It contains also several spinning-mills, bleacheries, copper works, tanneries, paper-mills, and 3 printing establishments. Its commerce, which is considerable, consists chiefly in articles of local produce.—C. was founded in 647, and derives its name from a Roman camp which was in the vicinity. It suffered much from the religious wars of the 16th cent.; and its fortifications were ultimately destroyed by Louis XIII. -Also a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. of Labrède, 13 m. SE of Bordeaux, near the l. bank of the Garonne. Pop. 755. The environs afford good wine.
CASTREZZATO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, prov. and 13 m. WSW of Brescia, district and 4 m. SE of Chiari. Pop. 1,810. It contains some iron-works.
CASTRICOM'S BAY, an indentation of the NE coast of the island of Celebes, in N lat. 0° 50'. CAS
CASTRICUN, or KASTRIKUM, a town of Holland, in the prov. of N Holland, 7 m. SSW of Alkmaar, and 17 m. NW of Amsterdam. It was taken by the English in 1799.

CASTRIES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Herault, arrond. of Montpellier. The cant. comprises 20 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,808; in 1841, 6,717.—The town is 8 m. NE of Montpellier. Pop. 715. It contains a fine Gothic castle. The environs afford good wine.

CASTRIES, or Casters (Bay of), a bay on the E coast of Chinese Tartary, situated in the channel or gulf of Tartary, separating that part of the continent from the island or peninsula of Sagalien, in N lat. 51° 30'. Here the time of full water at new and full moon is 10 o'clock, when its greatest rise is 5 ft. 8 inches, and the current runs half-a-mile an hour. The surrounding country is mountainous, and so totally covered with wood that the whole coast is an entire forest. Various kinds of volcanic substances are abundant on the shores, and among the islets at the entrance of the bay. During the month of August, while M. de la Perouse anchored here, the therm. in the open air stood invariably at 66°; but the heat did not penetrate the earth, which appeared to be frozen throughout the year at a certain depth. The heat of running water was ascertained never to exceed 41°; that of the casks remained at 36°. A small kind of grain cultivated here, and much esteemed by the inhabitants, was said to have come from the country of the Mantshus, about seven or eight days' journey distant. Few living animals, either quadrupeds or birds, were seen by Perouse's expedition; but fish seemed plentiful on the coast. The only animals domesticated were dogs, which resemble the wolf-dog. Population appeared to be thinly disseminated; and the inhabitants were of short stature, and more feeble conformation than those of Sagalien. Their size was below 4 feet 10 inches; their persons slender, and their voices feeble. They were extremely mild and affable, testified no distrust of strangers, and practised the most scrupulous honesty, though of filthy habits, and living in great destitution. They chiefly subsist on fish, particularly salmon, which they devour raw with extraordinary avidity; and collect wild roots for winter provision
CASTRIGNANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra di Otranto, district and 27 m. SE of Gallipoli, and 22 m. S of Otranto. Pop. 1,117.

CASTRIL, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 65 m. NE of Granada, partido and 7 m. WNW of Huescar, on the l. bank of the stream and E side of the mountain of the same name. Pop. 1,910.—The Sierra-C. runs NNE, and unites with the Sierra-Sagra on the confines of Murcia.
CASTRILLO, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. of Zamora, partido of Fuente-el-Sanco, on the l. bank of the Guerina.
CASTRILLO-DE-SAN-JUAN, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 24 m. SE of Palencia, on the l. bank of the Esgueva.
CASTRILLO-DE-DUERO, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 40 m. ESE of Valladolid, partido and 5 m. E of Penatiel, between the Duero and Duraton.

CASTRILLO-DE-LA-REINA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 30 m. SE of Burgos, partido of Salas-de-los-Infantes, near the l. bank of the Arlanza. Pop. 701, chiefly agricultural. It contains a church and a custom-house.
CASTRILLO-DE-LAS-PIEDRAS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 26 m. SW of Leon, partido and 5 m. SE of Astorga, near the r. bank of the Tuerto.

CASTRILLO-DE-LA-VEGA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 50 m. SSW of Burgos, partido and 5 m. WSW of Aranda de Duero, near the l. bank of the Duero. Pop. 588. It contains a church and custom-house.
CASTRILLO-DE-MURCIA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 29 m. W of Burgos, partido and 5 m. N of Castrojeriz.
CASTRILLO-DE-ONIELO, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 11 m. E of Palencia, partido of Baltanas-y-Dehesa-de-Valverde, on a height near an affluent of the Pisuerga.
CASTRILLO-DE-PORMA, a town in the prov., partido, and 11 m. ENE of Leon, near the l. bank | prov. of Beira, comarca and 20 m. SSW of Lamego. of the Curueno.
CASTRILLO - DE - VILLAVEGA, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 29 m. N of Palencia and partido of Saldanha.
CASTRILLOTE-JERIEGO, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 17 m. ENE of Valladolid, partido of Villalon, near the r. bank of the Jaramillo.

CASTRO, a village of the States-of-the-Church, in the delegation and 26 m. WNW of Viterbo, on the r. bank of the Olpeta. This was formerly a town of considerable importance, and seat of a bishopric. It was destroyed by Pope Innocent X. in 1649.— Also a village in the delegation and 12 m. SE of Frosinone, on a rising ground.—Also a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Otranto, district and 30 m. ESE of Gallipoli, on the Adriatic. Pop. 132. It contains an old castle and a cathedral, and has a port capable of admitting small vessels. The environs, which are extremely fertile, produce wine, grain, fruit, and cotton. This town is of great antiquity, and was formerly the seat of a bishopric. In the 16th century it was ravaged by the Turks, and has frequently since suffered from invasions of the Barbaresques corsairs.—Also a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, district of and on the Blegno, 15 m. NNE of Bellinzona. Pop. (Cath.) 153.—Also a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 27 m. NNE of Almeria, and partido of Gergal.—Also a town in Aragon, in the prov. and 42 m. E of Huesca, partido and 14 m. NE of Barbastro, near the r. bank of the Essera.
CASTRO, a town on the island of Imbro, in the Grecian archipelago, on a stream which flows into a bay of the S coast.

CASTRO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. and 232 m. WSW of São-Paulo, comarca and 78 m. NW of Curitiba. It was originally a village of the Guarapuaba tribe, and named Japo or Hiapo from the river on which it is situated. The district is of great extent, and is bounded on the S and W by the prov. of Santa-Catherina and São-Pedro-do-Rio-Grande. It affords excellent pasturage to great numbers of horses, mules, and sheep, and produces good timber. Precious stones are found in some of its localities. -Also a town of Chile, in the prov. and on the E coast of the island of Chiloe, 40 m. S of San Carlos, and 200 m. SSW of Valdivia. Pop. 150. It possesses a port; and was formerly the cap. of the island. It at present consists of two or three short streets of wooden houses, and two churches both nearly in ruins.
CASTROBOL, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 45 m. NW of Valladolid, and partido of Valoria-la-Buena, on the l. bank of the Cea.
CASTROBOM, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca of Trancoso, 20 m. SE of Pinhel, on the Coa.

CASTRO-CALVON, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 35 m. SSW of Leon, partido of Baneza, on the Baldeira, which is here crossed by a bridge. Pop. 892. It is of considerable antiquity, and contains an old fortress.
